text.title: ADRs and LLM-First Workflow Trends
text.summary: ADR industry adoption, lifecycle, storage pattern, and AWS best practices; LLM-first adoption metrics, context packing, spec-first workflow, and MCP as standard agent protocol.

section.title: Architecture Decision Records

rule.statement: ADRs are append-only: never edit an accepted ADR; create a new one that supersedes the old decision.
rule.rationale: ADRs are the decision history. Editing them erases the reasoning trail future engineers use to understand why the system is the way it is. Agents asked to "clean up" docs may delete ADRs — explicitly prohibit deletion in executor prompts.

section.p: ADR lifecycle: Draft → Review → Accepted → [Superseded | Deprecated]. Storage pattern: docs/adr/0001-use-postgresql.md.

list.item: ADR standard practice at: AWS, Google Cloud, Microsoft Azure, UK Government
Separate design doc from ADR (reference, not embed)
Push for timely decisions (most are two-way doors)

rule.statement: Tag any roadmap decision citing this trends document as "trend-based assumption" since market share statistics (Backstage 89%, adoption rates) become stale within 6-12 months.
rule.rationale: Trend data has implied precision it does not have. ADRs citing trend data without source dates and caveats mislead future engineers who encounter the record without temporal context.

section.title: LLM-First Workflow Adoption
section.p: Adoption metrics as of 2025 (directional, verify currency):

list.item: 65% of developers use AI coding tools weekly (Stack Overflow)
25% of Y Combinator W25 codebases are 95%+ AI-generated
~25% of Microsoft and Google code is AI-generated

rule.statement: Do not treat "90% of Claude Code's code is written by Claude Code itself" as a quality target; it is a marketing claim, not a benchmark.
rule.rationale: Agents citing adoption statistics as quality targets produce overconfident recommendations. The metric measures output volume, not quality or correctness.

list.item: Context packing: brain-dump everything the model should know; include high-level goals, invariants, examples of good solutions, approaches to avoid
Spec-first workflow: write detailed spec before any code; break work into small testable chunks; provide extensive codebase context; review everything generated
Incremental generation: generate small, testable chunks rather than entire features at once

rule.statement: Use MCP (Model Context Protocol) for agent-tool integration — it has become the standard protocol for agent interactions as of 2025.
rule.rationale: MCP components: Servers (tool providers), Clients (AI agents), Resources (context data), Tools (callable functions). Using MCP-compatible tools ensures agents work with a standard interface rather than custom integrations per tool.

list.item: Context window limits: mitigate with chunking, RAG, better prompts
Hallucination: mitigate with human review gates, testing
Spec drift: mitigate with continuous validation against spec.md
Non-determinism: mitigate with CI/CD quality gates
